annotations.label: Non-existence implies non-zero oscillation

anchor.snippet: If the limit does not exist, then the oscillation of f at p is non-zero

annotations.note: Mathlib has `oscillation` and the equivalence `oscillation f x = 0 ↔ ContinuousAt f x`, from which the article's contrapositive follows, but the exact stated implication isn't a separate lemma.

anchor.snippet: the limit of f , as x approaches p from values in T , is L

annotations.note: Limits along a subset are expressed in Mathlib as `Tendsto f (𝓝[T] p) (𝓝 L)` using `nhdsWithin`.

anchor.snippet: The non-deleted limit of f , as x approaches p , is L if

annotations.label: Sin(1/(x-1)) has no limit at 1

anchor.snippet: has no limit at any x -coordinate

annotations.label: Limit as x approaches infinity

anchor.snippet: The limit of f as x approaches infinity is L

annotations.note: Limits at +∞ are formalized as `Tendsto f atTop (𝓝 L)` using the `Filter.atTop` filter.

annotations.label: Limit as x approaches minus infinity

anchor.snippet: the limit of f as x approaches minus infinity is L

annotations.note: Limits at -∞ are formalized as `Tendsto f atBot (𝓝 L)` using the `Filter.atBot` filter.

annotations.note: Expressed in Mathlib as `Tendsto f (𝓝 p) atTop` using the `atTop` filter on the codomain.

annotations.label: Infinite limit at a point (−∞)

anchor.snippet: the limit of f as x approaches p is minus infinity

annotations.note: Expressed as `Tendsto f (𝓝 p) atBot` using the `atBot` filter on the codomain.

annotations.label: Limits at infinity of rational functions

anchor.section: Limits at infinity for rational functions
anchor.snippet: There are three basic rules for evaluating limits at infinity for a rational function

annotations.note: The three cases (deg P < deg Q, equal, greater) are formalized via `div_tendsto_atTop_zero_of_degree_lt`, `div_tendsto_atTop_leadingCoeff_div_of_degree_eq`, and `div_tendsto_atTop_of_degree_gt'`.

annotations.label: Limit at infinity gives horizontal asymptote

anchor.section: Limits at infinity for rational functions
anchor.snippet: If the limit at infinity exists, it represents a horizontal asymptote at y = L

annotations.note: Mathlib has limits at infinity but does not define `HorizontalAsymptote` as a named concept.

annotations.label: Limit of a two-variable function

anchor.snippet: the limit of f as ( x , y ) approaches ( p , q ) is L

annotations.note: Two-variable ordinary limits are `Tendsto f (𝓝 (p, q)) (𝓝 L)` using the product topology's neighborhood filter.

annotations.label: Multivariable limit existing at (0,0)

anchor.snippet: such that for all real x ≠ 0 and real y ≠ 0

annotations.note: Specific worked example with no Mathlib counterpart found.

annotations.label: Function with no limit at (0,0)

anchor.snippet: does not have a limit at (0, 0)

annotations.note: Specific counterexample, not stated as a Mathlib lemma.

annotations.label: Double (multiple) limit

anchor.snippet: the double limit of f as x approaches p and y approaches q is L

annotations.note: The double/multiple limit is `Tendsto f (𝓝 p ×ˢ 𝓝 q) (𝓝 L)`, with the product filter supplied by `Filter.instSProd`.

annotations.label: Ordinary limit implies multiple limit

anchor.snippet: if the ordinary limit exists and equals L , then the multiple limit exists and also equals L

annotations.note: Mathlib has the relationship between product and neighborhood filters (`nhds_prod_eq` and `tendsto_prod_iff`), from which this implication follows, but it's not stated as a standalone lemma.

annotations.label: Double limit at +∞

anchor.snippet: the double limit of f as x and y approaches infinity is L

annotations.note: Expressed as `Tendsto f (atTop ×ˢ atTop) (𝓝 L)` via the product filter from `Filter.instSProd`.

annotations.label: Double limit at −∞

anchor.snippet: the double limit of f as x and y approaches minus infinity is L

annotations.note: Expressed as `Tendsto f (atBot ×ˢ atBot) (𝓝 L)` via the product filter from `Filter.instSProd`.

annotations.label: Pointwise limit

anchor.snippet: the pointwise limit of f as x approaches p is g

annotations.note: Pointwise convergence is characterized by `tendsto_pi_nhds`: `Tendsto F l (𝓝 g) ↔ ∀ i, Tendsto (fun x => F x i) l (𝓝 (g i))` in the product topology.

anchor.snippet: has a uniform limit of constant zero function

annotations.note: Iterated limits as a named concept (`lim_{x→p} lim_{y→q} f(x,y)`) are not given a dedicated definition in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Limit theorem for vector-valued functions

anchor.snippet: the limit theorem for vector-valued function states that if the limit of each component exists

annotations.note: Componentwise convergence iff convergence in the product topology is exactly `tendsto_pi_nhds`.

annotations.label: Limit under the Manhattan metric

anchor.snippet: Then, under the Manhattan metric

annotations.note: `PiLp 1 fun i => ℝ` provides the Manhattan (L¹) metric instance; convergence reduces to the usual product convergence.

annotations.label: Uniform limit via uniform metric

annotations.note: Uniform convergence under the supremum/uniform metric is `TendstoUniformlyOn`.

annotations.label: Limit in topological spaces

anchor.snippet: there exists an open punctured neighbourhood

annotations.note: Mathlib's `Tendsto f (𝓝[≠] p) (𝓝 L)` (deleted) and `Tendsto f (𝓝 p) (𝓝 L)` (undeleted) define topological limits via neighborhood filters.

annotations.label: Sequential limit

anchor.snippet: There is another type of limit of a function, namely the sequential limit

annotations.note: Sequential limits are `Tendsto u atTop (𝓝 a)` for `u : ℕ → X`.

annotations.label: Topological limit implies sequential limit

anchor.snippet: however, the converse need not hold in general

annotations.note: If `Tendsto f (𝓝 p) (𝓝 L)` and `Tendsto x_n atTop (𝓝 p)`, then `Tendsto (f ∘ x_n) atTop (𝓝 L)` by `Tendsto.comp`.

annotations.label: Heine definition (sequential)

anchor.snippet: for all sequences x n (with, for all n , x n not equal to a ) converging to a the sequence f ( x n ) converges to L

annotations.note: `tendsto_iff_seq_tendsto` gives the Heine sequential characterization for countably generated filters (covering first-countable / metric spaces).

annotations.label: Non-standard calculus definition of limit

anchor.snippet: In non-standard calculus the limit of a function is defined by:

annotations.note: Mathlib has no nonstandard-analysis hyperreal/infinitesimal framework that would express this definition.

annotations.label: Nearness definition of limit

anchor.snippet: A point x is defined to be near a set

annotations.note: No nearness-space (Herrlich-style) limit definition was located in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Continuity at a point via limits

anchor.snippet: A function f is said to be continuous at c if it is both defined at c

annotations.note: `ContinuousAt f x` is defined as `Tendsto f (𝓝 x) (𝓝 (f x))`, exactly the limit-equals-value condition.

annotations.label: Limit equals both one-sided limits

anchor.snippet: If a function f is real-valued, then the limit of f at p is L if and only if both the right-handed limit and left-handed limit

annotations.note: From `nhdsLE_sup_nhdsGE : 𝓝[≤] a ⊔ 𝓝[≥] a = 𝓝 a` and `tendsto_sup`, the two-sided limit equals both one-sided limits iff they agree.

annotations.label: Continuity iff limit equals value

anchor.snippet: The function f is continuous at p if and only if the limit of f ( x ) as x approaches p exists and is equal to f ( p )

annotations.note: This is the definition of `ContinuousAt` in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Linearity of the limit operation

anchor.snippet: If N is a normed vector space , then the limit operation is linear

annotations.note: Linearity follows from `Tendsto.add`, `Tendsto.const_smul`, etc., available for topological additive groups and modules.

annotations.label: Algebraic limit theorem

anchor.snippet: This fact is often called the algebraic limit theorem

annotations.note: Mathlib provides `Tendsto.add`, `Tendsto.mul`, `Tendsto.sub`, `Tendsto.div`, etc., collectively giving the algebraic limit theorem.

annotations.label: Chain rule for limits of compositions

anchor.snippet: this " chain rule " does hold if one of the following additional conditions holds

annotations.note: `Tendsto.comp` gives composition of limits at the filter level, which subsumes the article's conditional chain rule (continuity at the inner limit point ensures the side condition).

annotations.label: Limit of a rational function at infinity

anchor.snippet: For n a nonnegative integer and constants

annotations.note: The case-analysis on degree (lt/eq/gt) for rational function limits at infinity is given by the `Polynomial.div_tendsto_atTop_*` family of lemmas.

annotations.label: L'Hôpital's rule

anchor.snippet: This rule uses derivatives to find limits of indeterminate forms 0/0 or ±∞/∞

annotations.note: `Mathlib.Analysis.Calculus.LHopital` formalizes multiple variants of L'Hôpital's rule for 0/0 and ±∞/∞ indeterminate forms.

annotations.label: Limits as shorthand for sums and integrals

anchor.snippet: Specifying an infinite bound on a summation or integral is a common shorthand for specifying a limit

annotations.note: Mathlib uses `HasSum`/`tsum` and improper-integral limits (via `Tendsto` on `atTop`), so this notational shorthand is implicit in those definitions rather than a standalone item.

annotations.label: Indeterminate forms

anchor.snippet: called an indeterminate form , does not allow one to determine the result

annotations.note: Mathlib treats indeterminate forms operationally via L'Hôpital and limit-arithmetic lemmas, but does not define `IndeterminateForm` as a named concept.

annotations.label: Uniqueness of limit in Hausdorff space

anchor.snippet: the requirement that [MATH] be a Hausdorff space can be relaxed to the assumption that [MATH] be a general topological space, but then the limit of a function may not be unique

annotations.note: `tendsto_nhds_unique` states that in a T2 (Hausdorff) space, if `Tendsto f l (𝓝 a)` and `Tendsto f l (𝓝 b)` with `l ≠ ⊥`, then `a = b`.

annotations.label: Continuity at limit point characterized by limit

anchor.snippet: A function is continuous at a limit point

annotations.note: `continuousAt_iff_punctured_nhds` characterizes `ContinuousAt f a` as `Tendsto f (𝓝[≠] a) (𝓝 (f a))`, the limit-from-punctured-nhds form.
